title: How to Talk to Someone You’re Attracted To (Don’t Ask Questions!) – According to Machiavelli
text: Give me 37 seconds to convince you why not watching this video in full could be the worst decision of your life. Most people think that asking questions is the way to keep the conversation alive. But Machaveli, the man who taught kings how to hold power, once wrote that the one who asks obeys. And that's exactly why so many dates feel like job interviews instead of sparks flying. Because the second you hide behind safe questions like, "What do you do?" you've already surrendered. And if you don't catch yourself, you'll lose the spark before the conversation even begins. So, in this video, I'm going to show you three ways to talk to your crush, as if Matthew himself were teaching you how to win over a kingdom. Most people think asking questions is the easiest way to keep the conversation alive. But that actually kills attraction. It's easy to fall back on those safe questions like, "What do you do for fun?" But all that does is push the weight of the conversation onto the other person. Suddenly, it feels less like a date and more like a job interview. Anyone can do that. What creates real attraction isn't asking, it's inferring. When you infer, you make educated guesses about someone based on what they give you, their vibe, how they look, what they've told you. And this does three really powerful things all at once. It makes the conversation more fun because guessing is playful. It makes it more dangerous because you put skin in the game. You can be wrong, which raises tension. And it makes you more present because you're not waiting to fire off the next canned question. you're actually observing. For example, instead of asking, "Do you like your job?" You could notice the way that they roll their eyes when work comes up and point to their computer screen and say, "You look like you're over it." That's an inference, and it's playful. It sparks emotion and makes the other person want to respond. Machio once wrote, "Men in general judge more by the eye than by the hand. For everyone can see, but few can touch." In other words, most people just notice the surface. They they notice what they see. They take things at face value and they stop there. They ask the boring questions over and over again. That's

You start sounding like a rare person who actually sees. That's what creates tension, spark, and presence. The point: Attraction just dies when you're collecting facts. But it grows when you take the risk and say, "Hey, here's what I noticed about you." Before I move on, I want to tell you about something that I've been working on quietly for the past year. On October 1st, in one week, I'm releasing my own handmade fragrance. I didn't make it because it was trendy. I made it because I needed it. I wanted something unique that only a few people in the world would have. Something with real quality, something that would that would leave people thinking about me after I left a room. The kind of smell that makes you think of a fall that you'll never forget or a person that you can't stop remembering. I only made like a hundred bottles and when they're gone, they're completely gone. You can click the link in my bio to put your number in there and I'll text you when the site is open. Number two, have you ever seen those Japanese motorbikes that can rev up to crazy RPMs? Being attractive is just as simple as turning the bike's handle and revving up your engine. Having the ability to rev your personality up in an instant to crazy RPMs is one of the most underrated and secret techniques in dating and attraction. Because the truth is, you're not always going to feel your best. Some days you're tired or you're stressed or you're caught up in your own head. But in a world full of people who hate their jobs, hate their friends, hate their life, who commute slowly through life like half dead engines, the person who revs up, who who listens with real presence, who who fills a room with laughter. That person wakes other people up from their slumber, from the monotony of everyday life. Matthew writing 500 years ago, noticed something simple but powerful. text: r slumber, from the monotony of everyday life. Matthew writing 500 years ago, noticed something simple but powerful. The people who move others aren't the cautious ones. They're the ones who act in boldness and take risk. He famously wrote, "Fortune favors the bold." And that's the same in conversation. People aren't drawn to hesitation. They're drawn to a spark. Person who brings life into a room, who shakes them out of autopilot, who makes them feel more alive just by being there. Looks may turn someone's head in the moment, but energy is what makes people remember you. Once you kind of got a little momentum in a conversation, you're going to be met with challenges and disagreement. And your initial reaction could be, "Oh no, it's over. This person doesn't like me." But what if I told you that that is wrong? Disagreement is good in a conversation. And it means that you're worth giving an opinion to, a real opinion, a real thought. Not being met with disagreement is what I call being hit with onion. An onion, like a conversation, has a lot of layers. And it's bad when you get hit with the surface layer because the surface layer is full of small talk and friend zones. You want to go deeper and you want to occupy those deeper layers of the conversational onion. And to do that, your crush is going to naturally challenge you and challenge your frame to see if you're worth being there. Attraction doesn't grow when you comply. It grows when you hold tension and you play with it. And Machaveli would back this up. In the prince, he wrote, "It's much safer to be feared than love if one must choose. Not because cruelty wins hearts, but because people respect strength more than surrender. In conversation, the same principle, it applies. When someone pushes back, it's not rejection, it's a test. If you collapse and throw away your opinion, you lose weight. But if you hold your frame or even double down with humor, you prove that you're worth taking seriously. That's why small talk, it dies on the surface. But playful disagreement, it feels alive. Attraction, it doesn't grow in compliance. It grows in tension. in the play between two strong people, between two strong frames. So, in the end, most people lose their crush's interest within the first sentence. text: crush's interest within the first sentence. Not because they're not good enough, but because they play it safe. They hide behind small talk and low energy and quick agreement. The truth is, connection and attraction doesn't come from being perfect. It comes from being alive in the moment, making inferences, and bringing energy, holding tension. Your crush is not going to remember the facts that you traded back and forth. They'll remember the spark that you brought to the room. And if you do that, you won't just be noticed.
